title: Scandinavian rosettes
subtitle: Crisp, deep-fried batter cookies made with a hot rosette iron and dusted with icing sugar.
excerpt: Scandinavian rosettes are thin, crisp fried cookies made by dipping a specially shaped hot iron into a light batter, then lowering it into hot oil. The batter sets around the iron in seconds and slides off as a delicate shell. They are dusted with icing sugar while still warm. A rosette iron is essential; the recipe cannot be made without one.
sourceNotes: Rosette cookies are traditional in Scandinavia, Germany, and the Baltic states, particularly at Christmas and Mardi Gras. The technique spread to North America through Scandinavian and German immigrants, particularly in the Upper Midwest. The iron mould gives the batter its characteristic snowflake or flower shape. The Swedish version, struvor, is made at Christmas; the Norwegian version, rosetter, at Carnival. The batter is simple: eggs, milk, flour, and a little sugar, producing a neutral canvas for icing sugar and sometimes cinnamon.

content.text: A rosette iron is a metal mould (usually a flower or star) on a long handle. You heat it in the oil, dip it into the batter just to the top of the mould, return it to the oil, and within 20 to 30 seconds the batter crisps and releases. The iron must be hot before it goes into the batter; a cold iron means the batter runs off instead of sticking.
Rest the batter for at least 30 minutes before frying. The resting time lets the flour fully hydrate and reduces the number of air bubbles, which improves the texture of the finished rosette.

items.name: Ground cinnamon
items.prepNote: optional, mix with the icing sugar

content.text: Whisk {{eggs}}, {{whole-milk}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{vanilla-extract}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} together. Sift in {{plain-flour}} and whisk to a smooth, thin batter. Rest for at least 30 minutes.
Pour {{sunflower-oil}} into a deep saucepan or fryer to a depth of at least 8 cm. Heat to 190°C.

Submerge the rosette iron in the hot oil for 30 seconds until very hot. Lift and shake off excess oil, then dip immediately into the batter up to the top of the mould (not over the top, or the rosette will not release). Hold it there for 3 seconds.

Lower the coated iron back into the hot oil. The rosette will puff and start to brown after 15 to 20 seconds. Gently ease it off the iron with a fork if it has not released on its own. Fry for a further 10 to 15 seconds until pale golden. Remove with a slotted spoon or tongs.
Drain on kitchen paper. Dust generously with {{icing-sugar}} while still warm. If using {{cinnamon-ground}}, mix it into the icing sugar before dusting.
Reheat the iron in the oil between each rosette. The iron must be very hot each time.

attrs.intro: The iron temperature is the one thing that matters most.

items.fix: Return the iron to the oil and heat for a further 30 seconds. The iron must be very hot when it enters the batter.
items.symptom: Batter slides off the iron rather than sticking.

items.fix: Lower the temperature to 180°C and fry slightly longer.
items.symptom: Rosettes brown too fast on the outside but remain raw inside.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
